SOP OnBoardingOffboarding_0.chunk_text:
Onboarding & Offboarding Engineers
         Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 24th April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
This SOP document details the processes for onboarding and offboarding caregivers, focusing on maintaining team structure, providing necessary tool training, mentorship, and access to comprehensive training manuals. During onboarding, employees will receive an overview of team dynamics, essential tool training, and mentorship to integrate seamlessly into the organization. Offboarding procedures ensure a smooth transition through access revocation, effective knowledge transfer, and the removal of operation identifiers (OPIDs). By adhering to this SOP, teams can ensure both efficient onboarding for new employees and secure, well-organized offboarding processes, maintaining operational integrity and continuity.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Caregivers and Engineers in general.
Personnel: Engineering Managers, HR personals, IT Admins, Team Leads, Mentors .
Systems: All infrastructure components, applications, resources and services used by the organization; assets issued.
Exceptions
Third-party Services: Support and Contracts may be limited by contractual agreements or vendor-specific requirements.
Critical Incidents: In the event of a security incident, the incident response plan will take precedence over this SOP.
Key Definitions
[Provide explanations of key terms, abbreviations, or jargon used in the document.]
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
The following tools and technologies will be reviewed:
Azure DevOps\GitHub: – Code Repository
MS Teams, Slack, Email groups - Communication
Azure Wiki / SharePoint: – Documentation
Azure AD - IAM/Access Mgmt.
CyberArk, Key Vault,  Azure Monitor – Logs, passwords, auth
ServiceNow, Self-service, VPN – Access to issue catalogue

SOP OnBoardingOffboarding_1.chunk_text:
Custom Tools: – Used for specific monitoring needs, specific metrics required by different departments.
Procedure
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective data extraction:
ONBOARDING PROCEDURE: 
Preboarding & Setup
Assignment of OPID/ User Id 
Create email, AD account, and initial role provisioning
Add to distribution lists and channels
Team Introduction: Share org chart
Intro to team, mentor/buddy assigned
Environment Setup
Grant access to tools: repo, ticketing system, cloud environment
Configure workspace: IDEs, VPNs, VMs. Required access.
Training and Documentation
Provide manuals, SOPs, KT documents
HIPAA/SOC2/PCI policy orientation 
Probation and Monitoring 
30/60/90 days check ins
Feedback collection and access review 
OFFBOARDING PROCEDURE: 
Notice & Exit Kick-off:  
Manager notifies HR/IT
Initiate offboarding checklist
Knowledge Handover: 
Document open tasks
Transfer repo/project ownership
Final KT session (recorded/logged)
Access Revocation:
Disable OPID/Caregiver ID, AD account, MFA tokens
Revoke VPN, DB, repo
Remove from Teams, Jira, etc.
Hardware & Data Handoff: 
Collect laptops, tokens, smart cards
Transfer relevant files to manager/team
Audit & Compliance Closure:
Update asset logs
Close user sessions in SIEM tools
Final email archival / DLP checks
Post Departure: 
Account Monitoring (if necessary and legally permissible)
monitoring for unusual activity might occur if legally permissible
The focus remains on complete access termination to minimize security risks
Data Archiving and Retention
While adhering to privacy regulations
Updating Internal Records
HR systems and organizational charts are updated to reflect departure
Insights and Implications for Offboarding
Automations in place to reduce complexity and move fast
Managing Employee Transitions to Subsidiaries:
Triggering the Transition Protocol:

SOP OnBoardingOffboarding_2.chunk_text:
The transition protocol starts with formal notification to HR from Employee or Manager about transfer with effective dates.
Immediate Termination of Access to Providence 
Including but not limited to:
System Access
Physical Access
Data Access
Communication and Coordination
Setting up new accounts in subsidiary 
Prevention of Overlapping Access ("Ghost IDs"):
Verification of Complete Access Disablement
Distinct Email Addresses and User IDs
Strict Policy Against Credential Reuse
Insights and Implications for Subsidiary Transfers awareness
Prevention of "Ghost IDs" and Dormant Accounts:
Practices to be followed
Using Robust Identity and Access Management (IAM) System
Regular Audits and Monitoring of User Accounts and Access Privileges
Enforcement of the Principle of Least Privilege
Automations and Monitoring 
Automated De-provisioning Workflows
Integration Between HR and IT Systems
Policy on Account Inactivity and Dormancy
Insights and Implications for Preventing Ghost IDs
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
 [Specify how success is measured]
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:
Update asset logs: This involves recording and updating information about organization's assets in a log or database.
Close user sessions in SIEM tools: Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) tools are used for monitoring and managing security-related data from across an organization. This helps prevent unauthorized access and enhances security.
Final email archival / DLP checks: This refers to the process of archiving emails and conducting Data Loss Prevention (DLP) checks.
Review & Maintenance 
Procedures will be reviewed every 3 months by the Security, IT and HR teams to ensure they are effective and efficient. 
Capturing proper feedback 
Updates reflected in version-controlled internal SOP document

SOP OnBoardingOffboarding_3.chunk_text:
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Term/Acronym | Definition
Principle of Least Privilege | Concept that a user should only have access to what they absolutely need to perform their responsibilities, and no more.
SOC 2 | System and Organization Controls (SOC) 2
OPID | "Operator Identifier." It is often used in contexts where individual operators within a system or network are assigned unique identifiers for tracking, authentication, or management purposes.   Caregiver ID can serve a similar purpose here.
Ghost ID | identifier or profile that remains in the system after the employee has left.
Role | Responsibility
Engineering Managers | Ensure seamless technical integration and resource allocation for new and departing employees.
Team Leads | Facilitate team-specific orientation and transition processes for incoming and outgoing staff.
HR & IT Administrators | Oversee documentation, access management, and policy adherence during onboarding and offboarding.
Security & Compliance Officers | Ensure adherence to security protocols and compliance standards for new hires and leavers.
Mentors / Buddies (for onboarding) | Provide guidance and support to newcomers, aiding their smooth transition into the organization.
Role | Responsibility
Team Leads | Responsible for implementing policy and SOP in their portfolio of applications and ensuring well documented transfer or inclusion. Ensures no flaw in transition process
Managers | Responsible for ensuring smooth transitions from technical as well as legal standpoint
IT Admins | Responsible for instrumenting applications and providing necessary setup and requirements
Application Owners, Analysts | Responsible for the access control of Knowledgebase and application delegation
Metric | Target / Description
Onboarding time (days) | The duration, in days, from when a new employee is hired to when they are fully onboarded and operational.

SOP OnBoardingOffboarding_4.chunk_text:
Offboarding completion (hours/days) | The total time, in hours or days, required to complete the offboarding process for an employee, including revoking access, returning company property, and completing necessary documentation.
Access provisioning delay | The time taken to grant necessary system and tool access to employees, measured from request initiation to access completion.
Compliance closure rate | The percentage of compliance-related tasks and audits that are completed successfully within a given timeframe.
KT handoff documentation score | The quality score of Knowledge Transfer (KT) handoff documentation, assessing how comprehensive, clear, and useful the documentation is in facilitating seamless transitions and knowledge continuity within teams.

SOP Data Migration_0.chunk_text:
Data Migration
Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 25th April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
The purpose of this document is to outline the procedures and best practices for Data Migration while ensuring data accuracy, security and minimal business disruption. It provides guidelines for source/target mapping, volume benchmarking, dry runs, validation checks and cutover plans. The goal is to minimize rework due to missed mappings, data loss and prolonged downtime during migration.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Data Engineering teams
Personnel: All team members responsible for code development, support and maintenance of respective data platforms.
Systems: All infrastructure components, applications, devices and services used by the organization.
Exceptions
Third-party Services: Monitoring and support for third-party services may be limited by contractual agreements or vendor-specific requirements.
Legacy Systems: Legacy systems not currently supported by the infrastructure team may require separate procedures or exemptions.
Security Incidents: In the event of a security incident, the incident response plan will take precedence over this SOP.
Key Definitions
[Provide explanations of key terms, abbreviations, or jargon used in the document.]
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
The following tools and technologies can be utilized to support data migration:
Azure Data Factory, Databricks, Python - For data extraction and creation of migration and testing scripts.
Delta tables/Azure Synapse – As target DB
Airflow/ADF – For Orchestration of data pipelines
Azure Data Lake – For intermediate data storage/staging
Azure DevOps/GitHub – Code Repository
Azure Wiki – Documentation
Application Insights – For performance monitoring, tracking requests, exceptions, and dependencies.
Log Analytics – For collecting, analyzing, and visualizing logs 
Pre-requisites

SOP Data Migration_1.chunk_text:
Establish connectivity between source, intermediate storage and destination data platforms
Data classification
Procedure
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective data migration:
Source/Target Mapping:
Identify and document all source systems. 
Create data catalog to define source data and field-to-field mapping document from each schema/database/table to target.
Define target database, schema, tables, columns, datatypes and constraints.
Define data transformation rules, if required, along with validation rules. 
Define rules for Change Data Capture (CDC).
Identify dependencies within and across source systems to determine the sequence of data movement for maintaining data integrity.
Identify redundancies in source data and opportunities to reduce data duplication in target system.
Volume Benchmarking:
Assess total volume of data to be migrated and history versus daily data load volumes in source system. 
Define maximum volume of data transfer during each batch, based on system performance and network capacity. 
Checkpointing of data extracted and migrated to avoid duplication/missing data.
Validation Checks:
Setup both automated and manual data validation checks at every stage of migration. 
Setup processes to validate data continuity and integrity across each batch load, to avoid missing or corrupt data.
Ensure compliance with data security and confidentiality standards.
Get sign-offs from stakeholders on data to be migrated before moving to production environment.
Dry Runs:
Conduct dry runs for data extraction from source and data loads to target in non-prod environments and extrapolate performance on prod environment as per infrastructure configuration. 
Perform data accuracy and completeness checks on migrated data. 
Address any data or performance issues identified during these dry runs.
Cutover Plan:
Define the cutover strategy with clear timelines and ensure readiness of the target system.  
Define a clear rollback procedure. 
Notify stakeholders.
Monitor migration progress and perform real-time checks on data and system stability.
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
 [Specify how success is measured]
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:

SOP Data Migration_2.chunk_text:
Organizational Policies: Comply with organizational policies, procedures, and guidelines.
Security Best Practices: Implement security best practices and guidelines.

SOP Data Migration_3.chunk_text:
Review & Maintenance 
Schedule ongoing data audits with stakeholders and data consumers to identify any data drifts.
Improvements will be made based on lessons learned and changing data requirements.
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Version 1.0 | 4/25/2025 | Initial draft | Ujwala Yadavalli, ESI
Term/Acronym | Definition
Mapping document | A document that defines how data from different source systems is related and transformed when moved to target
Data parity | Check if the data matches during transfer and storage.
CDC | Change Data Capture refers to identifying and tracking changes (like inserts, updates, deletes) to data in real-time/near real-time
Logs | Records of events that occur during data extraction, processing and loads
Role | Responsibility
Data Team Leads | Review for technical accuracy and feasibility.
Data Producers/Consumers | Review for application-specific requirements
Security Team | Review for security compliance
Role | Responsibility
Data Engineer | Responsible for creating data extraction and migration scripts, loading and validation
Data Architect | Responsible for creating a migration approach based on architecture of new system
DevOps Engineer | Responsible for setting up infrastructure, networking and access control
QA Engineer | Responsible for performing data validations and parity checks
PM | Responsible for coordination with stakeholders
Metric | Target / Description
Data Loss | 0% data loss or data corruption while data migration
Data Parity | 100% data parity on critical attributes
< 0.1% variation for non-critical attributes
Availability / Uptime | Zero unplanned downtime
System Response | Response time within <X> seconds for critical queries

SOP Requirement Intake_0.chunk_text:
Requirement Intake 
Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 28th April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
This SOP document outlines procedures for managing requirement intake. It provides guidelines for request logging, prioritization, fulfilment, validation, stakeholder alignment, impact assessment, and tracking. The goal is to ensure efficient handling of requests, minimize misaligned expectations, rework, missed deadlines, and scope creep. This is an automated requirement intake process designed to streamline operations and ensure timely and accurate fulfilment of requirements.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Data Engineering teams.
Personnel: All team members responsible for managing, maintaining, and supporting the requirement intake process.
Systems: All infrastructure components, applications, devices, and services used by the organization.
Exceptions
Third-party Services: Requirement intake for third-party services may be limited by contractual agreements or vendor-specific requirements.
Legacy Systems: Legacy systems not currently supported may require separate procedures or exemptions.
Security Incidents: In the event of a security incident, the incident response plan will take precedence over this SOP.
Key Definitions
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
The following tools and technologies will be utilized to support requirement intake:
Azure DevOps: Used for managing work items created from the intake form submissions.
Azure Dashboard: Utilized for tracking the status of requests.
SharePoint: Hosting intake form and documentation.
Lists Forms: Provides the intake form for submitting requests.
Power Automate: Automatically creates a work item (user story or bug) in Azure DevOps (ADO) and automated notifications. 
Custom Tools: Used for specific requirement intake needs, such as custom metrics or business-specific tracking.
Procedure

SOP Requirement Intake_1.chunk_text:
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective requirements intake:
1. Request Logging
Users can access the requirement intake form available on a publicly accessible SharePoint. The form includes multiple fields such as:
Request type (e.g., general support, access request, bug report)
Data warehouse
Brief description of the requirement
Detailed description
Department
Location
Contact details, etc.
Upon completion and submission, the form automatically creates a work item (user story or bug) in Azure DevOps (ADO) using Power Automate and mapped to a predefined parent based on the data warehouse and tagged accordingly (e.g., break fix, access request, bug).
After submission, an automated acknowledgement is sent to the requestor with details of the requirement, along with an automated email notification to the team informing them of the submission of a new requirement.

SOP Requirement Intake_2.chunk_text:
2. Prioritization and Impact Assessment
Requests are prioritized based on their urgency and impact. The prioritization criteria include business criticality, dependencies, and resource availability. 
High-priority requests are addressed first to ensure timely resolution of critical issues.
3. Fulfilment
Once prioritized, requests are assigned to the appropriate team members for fulfilment. 
The team follows a structured approach to address the requests, ensuring all necessary steps are taken to meet the requirements effectively.
4. Validation
After fulfilment, the requests undergo validation to ensure the solutions meet the specified requirements. 
Validation includes testing, review, and confirmation from the requestor that the issue has been resolved satisfactorily.
5. Stakeholder Alignment
Regular communication with stakeholders is maintained to ensure alignment on requirements, priorities, and progress. 
Stakeholders are kept informed through updates and tagging in the user story/bug, allowing them to track the status. Additionally, regular meetings foster transparency and collaboration.
6. ADO Board Tracking
The Azure DevOps board is used to track the status of the requests. 
The board categorizes requests into different states, types (e.g., access request, break fix) etc. This tracking ensures visibility throughout the process.
7. Backlog Grooming
Regular backlog grooming sessions are held to review and refine the list of pending requests. 
During these sessions, requests are reassessed for priority, relevance, and feasibility, ensuring the backlog remains manageable and up to date.
8. Stakeholder Approval
Before finalizing any request, stakeholder approval is obtained to confirm that the solution meets their expectations and requirements. 
This step ensures that all deliverables are aligned with stakeholder needs.
9. Definition of Done
The definition of done includes criteria that must be met for a request to be considered complete. 
These criteria typically involve successful validation, stakeholder approval, and documentation updates.
10. Tracking of Ad Hoc Data/Report Requests

SOP Requirement Intake_3.chunk_text:
Ad hoc data and report requests are tracked separately to ensure they are addressed promptly.
These requests are logged, prioritized, and fulfilled following the same structured approach as other requests.
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:
Organizational Policies: Comply with organizational policies, procedures, and guidelines.
Security Best Practices: Implement security best practices and guidelines.
Review & Maintenance 
Requirement intake procedures will be reviewed every 3 months by the Service/Data Engineering teams to ensure they are effective and efficient. Improvements will be made based on lessons learned and changing requirements.
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Term/Acronym | Definition
Requirement Intake | The process of logging, prioritizing, fulfilling, validating, and tracking requests within the Data Engineering team.
ADO Board | Azure DevOps dashboard used for tracking the status of the requests.
Backlog Grooming | Regular sessions to review and refine the list of pending requests.
Stakeholder Alignment | Regular communication with stakeholders to ensure alignment on requirements, priorities, and progress.
Role | Responsibility
End Users/Business Stakeholders | Submit requests and provide feedback on the fulfillment of their requirements.
Data Engineering Leads | Review of technical accuracy and feasibility of the requirements.
Data Engineering Team | Oversee the overall requirement intake process, ensuring alignment with data engineering goals and priorities.
Role | Responsibility
End Users | Submit requests through the intake form, provide detailed descriptions, and offer feedback on the fulfillment of their requirements.
Data Engineer | Responsible for managing the requirement intake process, including logging requests, configuring tools, performing routine checks, and responding to requests with corrective actions.
Metric | Target / Description
Request Fulfillment Rate | > 95% / Percentage of requests fulfilled within the agreed timeline
Backlog Reduction Rate | > 80% / Percentage reduction in backlog over a specified period

SOP Requirement Intake_4.chunk_text:
Request Rework Rate | < 10% / Percentage of requests requiring rework after initial fulfillment
Compliance Rate | 100% Adherence to organizational policies and security best practices

SOP Workflow Migration_0.chunk_text:
Workflow Migration
Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 28th April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
The purpose of this document is to outline a standardized approach for migrating data engineering workflows between platforms or environments, with a focus on maintaining data integrity, performance, and stakeholder satisfaction. The goal is to minimize disruption and data unavailability due to workflow migration.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Data Engineering teams
Personnel: All team members responsible for code development, support and maintenance of respective data platforms.
Systems: All infrastructure components, applications, devices and services used by the organization.
Exceptions
Third-party Services: Monitoring and support for third-party services may be limited by contractual agreements or vendor-specific requirements.
Legacy Systems: Legacy systems not currently supported by the infrastructure team may require separate procedures or exemptions.
Security Incidents: In the event of a security incident, the incident response plan will take precedence over this SOP.
Key Definitions
[Provide explanations of key terms, abbreviations, or jargon used in the document.]
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
For workflow migration, technology stack must be chosen based on below considerations: 
Data extraction, ingestion and processing
Workflow Orchestration
Scripting and tools for automated and manual testing
Code Repository
Log capturing
Performance monitoring
Documentation
Procedure
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective data migration:
Assessment:
Identify the workflows to be migrated.
For each workflow, analyse the data sources/sinks, and external dependencies.
Evaluate the compatibility of the new to-be used tool/platform with the sources/sinks, type of data, workflow orchestrator and dependencies identified in the above step.
Workflow Refactoring:
Setup and configure connections from source and to sink.

SOP Workflow Migration_1.chunk_text:
Configure permissions and secrets management.
Rewrite and re-engineer the workflow scripts, considering any deprecated functionalities/tools, to meet the requirements.
Establish CI/CD pipelines for deployment.
Workflow and Data Validations:
Setup data validations (for row count, checksum comparisons and aggregation tests) at each individual component/module and compare the behaviour and output with the legacy workflow.
Ensure end-to-end execution including data movement and integration with external components/systems.
Ensure data security at rest and transition.
Benchmark execution times, resource usage and cost.
Rollout Plan:
Perform dry runs to validate connection settings and configuration.
Create a rollback plan and define when to execute it.
Maintain backup of legacy code and configurations.
Perform parallel runs of legacy and new workflows, if required, to compare the data and system performance. Otherwise, disable the legacy workflow and trigger the new ones. 
Monitor the first few runs and validate the data and system performance.
Ensure workflow completion and data availability as per agreed SLA with stakeholders
Do not delete the legacy workflows unless the new workflows are stabilized and signoffs obtained from stakeholders.
Stakeholder Communication:
Schedule walkthroughs with stakeholders and data users to validate the data accuracy.
Provide required documentation on changes/enhancements to the new workflows.
Share the rollout plan and plan change management, as required.
Get approvals on SLA for data availability.
Capture feedback and change requests during UAT and production rollout, for further action.
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
 [Specify how success is measured]
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:
Organizational Policies: Comply with organizational policies, procedures, and guidelines.
Security Best Practices: Implement security best practices and guidelines.

SOP Workflow Migration_2.chunk_text:
Review & Maintenance 
Schedule ongoing data audits with stakeholders and data consumers to identify any data and performance drifts.
Improvements will be made based on lessons learned and changing data requirements.
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Version 1.0 | 4/28/2025 | Initial draft | Ujwala Yadavalli, ESI
Term/Acronym | Definition
Data parity | Check if the data matches during transfer and storage.
SLA | Service Level Agreement
Logs | Records of events that occur during data extraction, processing and loads
Role | Responsibility
Data Team Leads | Review for technical accuracy and feasibility.
Data Producers/Consumers | Review for application-specific requirements
Security Team | Review for security compliance
Role | Responsibility
Data Engineer | Responsible for code development
Data Architect | Responsible for creating architecture diagrams and migration approach
DevOps Engineer | Responsible for setting up infrastructure, networking and access controls
QA Engineer | Responsible for performing data validations and parity checks
PM | Responsible for communication with stakeholders
Metric | Target / Description
Data Parity | 100% data parity on critical workflows
< 1% variation for non-critical workflows
SLA | 100% adherence on data availability as per agreed SLA with stakeholders
Uptime | Zero unplanned downtime

SOP_Data Cataloging and Lineage_0.chunk_text:
Data Cataloging and Lineage
Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 23rd  April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
The purpose of this Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document is to provide a comprehensive guideline for data cataloging and lineage within the organization. It aims to ensure consistent, efficient, and accurate documentation of data assets, their origins, transformations, and movements across various systems and processes. This SOP serves as a guideline for managing and utilizing data catalogs to enhance data discoverability, accessibility, and understanding for all stakeholders. By implementing standardized practices for data lineage, the organization seeks to improve data governance, support regulatory compliance, facilitate impact analysis, and enable informed decision-making.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Data Governance, Data Engineering, and Data Analytics teams.
Personnel: All team members responsible for documenting, maintaining, and utilizing data catalogs and lineage information.
Systems: All databases, data warehouses, data lakes, and data integration platforms where data cataloging and lineage tracking are conducted within the organization.
Exceptions
Confidential Data: Cataloging and lineage tracking for data sets classified as confidential or sensitive may be restricted due to privacy regulations or security protocols.
Temporary Data Sets: Data assets created for short-term projects or testing purposes may not be subject to the standard documentation and tracking requirements outlined in this SOP.
Unsupported Tools: Data management systems and tools not officially endorsed or supported by the organization may require separate procedures or exemptions from this SOP.
Key Definitions
[Provide explanations of key terms, abbreviations, or jargon used in the document.]
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
The following tools and technologies will be utilized to support Data cataloguing and lineage:
Azure DevOps\GitHub – Code Repository

SOP_Data Cataloging and Lineage_1.chunk_text:
Azure Wiki/Sharepoint – Documentation
Custom/Vendor Tools – Used for data cataloging, such as metadata ingestion, lineage tracking, classifying data. Some of the vendor tools are Purview, Informatica, Collibra etc.
Procedure
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective Data Cataloging and lineage tracking:
Identify the Cataloging Tool:
Identify if any Enterprise data catalog is available either a Third party or In-House.
If not, then identify the specific needs and requirements of organization.
Research and evaluate different catalog tools to understand their features, benefits, limitations and alignment with Organization needs.
Setting up Scans: 
Identify the databases, data warehouses, data lakes, data integration platforms or reports for scans.
Setup a service account with appropriate access as per the tools to scan the system
The data governance team will set up and configure catalogs to automatically ingest metadata and lineage 
Setting up Lineage: 
Automated Lineage: Scan the database stored procs or the ETL/ELT with appropriate accesses to get automated lineage.
Manual Lineage: Set manual lineage by linking the assets.
Setting up Classifications
DG leads will identify the attributes that would be considered as PII, PHI or sensitive information.
The data governance team will configure classifications in the tool to identify PII, PHI or sensitive information.
Out of The Box Classification: These are the classification that are system defined
Custom Classification: Define the classification by using regex on Column name and data or using reference tables
Manual curation: Implement procedures for manual review and adjustment to address false positives or classification mismatches.
Role Assignment in Catalog:
Evaluate the responsibilities and access levels associated with each role in the catalog.
Identify individuals or teams for each role (Data Owner, Business Stewards, Technical Stewards, SME’s etc.)
Document them in the catalog and communicate the role assignment.
Create Glossaries and Data Dictionary:

SOP_Data Cataloging and Lineage_2.chunk_text:
Collaborate with domain experts and stakeholders to compile a list of terms, definitions, and data elements essential for the respective field. 
Develop relationships and examples to aid in interpreting and utilizing the glossary and data dictionary effectively.
Schedule Scans: Schedule the scans to automatically ingest any changes to the assets.
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
 [Specify how success is measured]
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:
Organizational Policies: Comply with organizational policies, procedures, and guidelines.
Security Best Practices: Implement security best practices and guidelines.

SOP_Data Cataloging and Lineage_3.chunk_text:
Review & Maintenance 
Data Catalogs will be reviewed every 6 months by the DG/Business teams to ensure they are effective and efficient. 
Improvements will be made based on lessons learned and changing infrastructure requirements.
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Term/Acronym | Definition
PII | PII, or Personally Identifiable Information, is data that can uniquely identify an individual, such as their name, address, phone number, or social security number.
PHI | PHI, or Protected Health Information, is any information about health status, provision of healthcare, or payment for healthcare that can be linked to an individual
Lineage | Lineage refers to the tracking and documentation of the origins and transformations of data as it flows through various systems
Metadata | Metadata is data that describes other data, providing information about its content, structure, context, and management to facilitate understanding and use.
Data Classification | Data classification is the process of organizing and categorizing data based on its sensitivity, value, and importance to ensure proper handling, security, and compliance.
Data Stewards | A business resource (or team) that owns and exercises decision rights regarding the appropriate use (policy-driven or business practice driven) of data in their sub-domain.
Glossary | A glossary is a list of terms and definitions related to a specific subject area, intended to clarify meaning and promote consistent understanding.
Data Dictionary | A data dictionary is a comprehensive resource that details the structure, characteristics, and relationships of data within a database, providing essential metadata for understanding and managing data.
Role | Responsibility
Data Governance Lead | Review for alignment with Data Management policies and regulatory compliance.
Data Governance Engineer | Review for technical accuracy and feasibility.
Role | Responsibility
Data Governance Lead | Responsible for overseeing data management policies, ensuring compliance, and driving the implementation of cataloging and lineage standards.

SOP_Data Cataloging and Lineage_4.chunk_text:
Data Governance Engineer | Responsible for providing technical support for data cataloging tools and ensures system integration, organizing data assets, and supporting the documentation processes.
Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) | Responsible for providing context to data by developing and maintaining business glossaries, ensuring that data assets are accurately described and understood within the organizational context.
Metric | Target / Description
Data Coverage | Percentage of organizational data assets accurately documented and catalogued.
Lineage Completeness | Extent to which data lineage is mapped, showing data flow from source to consumption
User Adoption Rate | Number of users actively engaging with the data cataloging and lineage tools.
Term Definition Completeness | Percentage of key terms and concepts that have comprehensive definitions in the glossary.

SOP_Data Governance and Security_0.chunk_text:
Data Governance and Security
Standard Operating Procedure
Version: 1.0
Date: 28th  April 2025
Owners
Document History
Purpose
The purpose of this Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document is to establish a comprehensive framework for data security within the organization for structured data at rest. It aims to protect sensitive information from unauthorized access and disclosure by outlining clear and consistent security measures. This SOP serves as a guideline for implementing robust data protection strategies, including encryption, access controls, and incident response protocols. By adhering to standardized practices for data security, the organization seeks to enhance its overall data governance, support regulatory compliance, safeguard stakeholder privacy, and maintain the integrity and confidentiality of its data assets.
Scope 
This SOP document applies to:
Departments: Data Governance, IT, and Compliance teams, as well as any other departments involved in data management and protection.
Personnel: All team members responsible for implementing, maintaining, and overseeing data governance and security protocols within the organization.
Systems: All organizational databases, data storage solutions, and any systems that handle or transmit sensitive data.
Processes: Procedures related to data access controls, encryption and incident response to ensure compliance and security.
Exceptions
Highly Sensitive Data: Additional security measures or exemptions may be required for data sets deemed highly sensitive, including proprietary or classified information, due to specific legal or regulatory requirements.
Legacy Systems: Older systems or technologies that cannot fully comply with current security standards may be subject to alternative governance and security procedures.
Third-Party Systems: Data managed by external vendors or third-party platforms may necessitate separate security protocols, in accordance with contractual agreements and external compliance regulations.
Unstructured or In-Transit Data: This SOP does not cover unstructured data or data that is in transit, as these may require separate processes for ensuring security.
Key Definitions
[Provide explanations of key terms, abbreviations, or jargon used in the document.]

SOP_Data Governance and Security_1.chunk_text:
Key Stakeholders for the SOP
The following key stakeholder individuals or teams involved in reviewing the SOP.
Responsibilities
The following roles and responsibilities are defined for individuals and teams involved in executing the SOP:
Tools and Technologies
The following tools and technologies will be utilized to support Data Governance and Security:
Azure DevOps\GitHub – Code Repository
Azure Wiki/Sharepoint – Documentation
Custom/Vendor Tools – Used for data protection, such as data classification, masking/encryption, access control and monitoring. Some of the vendor tools are Immuta, Okera, Privacera etc.
Procedure
The following procedures will be followed to ensure effective Data Governance and Security:
Identify the sensitive data assets:
DG leads will identify the attributes that would be considered as PII, PHI or sensitive information.
The data governance team will configure classifications in the tool to identify PII, PHI or sensitive information.
Implement procedures for manual review and adjustment to address false positives or classification mismatches.
Define Masking/Encryption Policy:
Choose suitable masking or encryption techniques for each classification level. For example, use data masking for confidential information that requires anonymity in non-production environments, and encryption for restricted data that must be protected during storage and transmission.
Identify Existing access and business justification:
Identify CGs presently currently have access to Personally Identifiable Information (PII), Protected Health Information (PHI), or other sensitive data, and verify whether there is an existing request supported by valid business justification.
Data Access Request and Approval workflow:
Establish a process that allows for CGs to gain access to the PII, PHI or sensitive data by submitting a request with business justification. The request must undergo an approval process that includes the CG's manager and the data owner as a minimum requirement.
Communicate the changes:
Prior to enacting any modifications to the access policy, inform affected users of the forthcoming changes and provide them with sufficient time to submit a new request if necessary.
Implement the access control:

SOP_Data Governance and Security_2.chunk_text:
Any of the below controls can be applied based on the requirements.
RBAC: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to assigns permissions to users based on their roles within an organization
ABAC: Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) grants access on the attributes based on user access rather than predefined roles.
RLS: Row-level security (RLS) restricts access to specific rows in a table based on user roles or attributes. 
Reporting: 
Effective data governance and security require comprehensive reporting and dashboarding to monitor, manage, and ensure compliance. Examples of reports and dashboards that can be created
Data Access Reports: Track who accessed specific data assets, when, and what actions were performed. These reports help monitor compliance with access policies and detect unauthorized access.
User Activity Monitoring: Track user interactions with data systems, highlighting suspicious behaviour or potential policy violations.
Compliance Dashboards: Summarize the organization's compliance status regarding data protection regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or CCPA. These dashboards highlight areas that need attention and track remediation efforts.
Set up Audits:
Define Audit Objectives: Clearly outline what will be achieved with the audit, such as compliance verification, risk assessment, or policy adherence.
Conduct Data Inventory and Classification: Compile a comprehensive inventory of data assets and classify them based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements to prioritize audit efforts.
Implement Monitoring Systems: Set up systems for continuous monitoring and logging of data activities. These systems provide valuable insights and evidence during the audit
Success Metrics & KPI’s 
 [Specify how success is measured]
Compliance & Audits 
The following compliance and audit requirements will be adhered to:
Organizational Policies: Comply with organizational policies, procedures, and guidelines.
Security Best Practices: Implement security best practices and guidelines.

SOP_Data Governance and Security_3.chunk_text:
Review & Maintenance 
Data Security will be reviewed every 3 months by the DG/Ops teams to ensure they are effective and efficient. 
Improvements will be made based on lessons learned and changing infrastructure requirements.
Name | Role | Group
Version | Revision Date | Document Revision Description | Author & Department | Approved By
Term/Acronym | Definition
PII | PII, or Personally Identifiable Information, is data that can uniquely identify an individual, such as their name, address, phone number, or social security number.
PHI | PHI, or Protected Health Information, is any information about health status, provision of healthcare, or payment for healthcare that can be linked to an individual
Sensitive data | Sensitive data is information that requires protection due to privacy, security, or regulatory reasons, beyond just PII and PHI, such as financial records and intellectual property.
Data Classification | Data classification is the process of organizing and categorizing data based on its sensitivity, value, and importance to ensure proper handling, security, and compliance.
Role | Responsibility
Data Governance Lead | Review for alignment with Data Management policies and regulatory compliance.
Data Governance Engineer | Review for technical accuracy and feasibility.
Role | Responsibility
Data Governance Lead | Responsible for overseeing data management policies, ensuring compliance, and driving the implementation of access controls and data classification.
Data Governance Engineer | Responsible for setting up automated data classification and their management.
Data Ops Engineer | Responsible for providing technical support for data protection and ensures appropriate accesses and support the documentation processes.
Metric | Target / Description
Data Access Requests | Number of data access requests approved versus denied
False Positive/Negative Rate | Frequency of incorrect classifications where data is mistakenly identified as sensitive or vice versa
Data Coverage Percentage | Proportion of the organization's data assets that have been successfully classified
Audit Findings | Number of audit findings related to data security issues
